tools.name: execute
tools.description:
Runs JavaScript code to interact with the Coingecko API.

You are a skilled TypeScript programmer writing code to interface with the service.
Define an async function named "run" that takes a single parameter of an initialized SDK client and it will be run.
For example:

```
async function run(client) {
  const price = await client.simple.price.get({ vs_currencies: 'usd', ids: 'bitcoin' });
}
```

You will be returned anything that your function returns, plus the results of any console.log statements.
Do not add try-catch blocks for single API calls. The tool will handle errors for you.
Do not add comments unless necessary for generating better code.
Code will run in a container, and cannot interact with the network outside of the given SDK client.
Variables will not persist between calls, so make sure to return or log any data you might need later.
Remember that you are writing TypeScript code, so you need to be careful with your types.
Always type dynamic key-value stores explicitly as Record<string, YourValueType> instead of {}.

intent.description: Task you are trying to perform. Used for improving the service.

tools.name: search_docs
tools.description: Search SDK documentation to find methods, parameters, and usage examples for interacting with the API. Use this before writing code when you need to discover the right approach.

language.description: The language for the SDK to search for.

detail.description: The amount of detail to return.

findings.title: Excessive description length in 'execute'
findings.description: Description is 1047 chars (limit 1000). May contain hidden content.
findings.remediation: Keep descriptions concise; review for hidden instructions.

findings.title: Shadow Tool — 'execute' conflicts with a high-value built-in name
findings.description: Tool 'execute' uses a name commonly associated with built-in or high-privilege operations. A malicious server could register this tool to shadow legitimate tools and intercept LLM actions.
findings.remediation: Rename the tool to something unique and context-specific. Avoid generic names like 'bash', 'execute', or 'read_file' that clash with well-known tool namespaces.

findings.title: initialize accepts protocol version downgrade
findings.description: The server accepted initialize with arbitrary protocol versions: ['9999-99-99', '2030-01-01', '1.0', '2024-01-01', '""', '0.1']. The server also accepted far-future versions, indicating no version validation at all. Servers should reject versions outside their supported range to prevent feature-downgrade attacks.
findings.remediation: Validate protocolVersion against a supported range. Return a JSON-RPC error for versions outside that range.

findings.title: Null request ID accepted — JSON-RPC spec violation
findings.description: The server accepted a request with id=null and returned a result. Per JSON-RPC 2.0, null IDs are reserved for notifications (no response expected). Accepting them may cause response routing bugs in complex orchestration scenarios.
findings.remediation: Reject requests with null id values; treat them as notifications.

findings.title: Protocol crash — string params caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a tools/call request where params is a string (not an object) caused the server to crash or disconnect. The JSON-RPC spec requires params to be an array or object.
findings.remediation: Validate params type (must be object or array) before processing. Return -32600 (Invalid Request) for wrong types.

findings.title: MCP server exposes tools with completable arguments
findings.description: 2 tool(s) have schema properties that may support completion/complete enumeration.
findings.remediation: Review which argument values are exposed via completion/complete.
